docs.title: Welcome to Vilnius 🇱🇹
docs.text:
Vilnius is the capital and largest city of Lithuania — a hidden gem of Europe where medieval cobblestones meet a vibrant modern culture. Founded in the 14th century, Vilnius boasts one of the largest surviving medieval Old Towns in Europe, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1994.

Quick Facts

  • Population: ~580,000 (city proper)
  • Language: Lithuanian (English widely spoken in tourist areas)
  • Currency: Euro (€)
  • Time Zone: EET (UTC+2), EEST (UTC+3 in summer)
  • Best time to visit: May–September

docs.title: What You'll Find Here
docs.text:
🗺️ Travel Guide

Everything you need before you arrive — visas, transport, budgeting, and practical tips.

Get started → 🏘️ Districts

Explore Vilnius neighbourhood by neighbourhood — from the Old Town to bohemian Užupis.

Explore districts → 🍽️ Food & Drink

Discover Lithuanian cuisine, the best restaurants, and the city's thriving coffee scene.

Discover food →

docs.title: Why Visit Vilnius?
docs.text:
Vilnius offers an incredible mix of experiences that punch well above its weight for a European capital:

  • Architecture — Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque, and Soviet all coexist in one walkable city
  • Affordability — significantly cheaper than Western European capitals
  • Food scene — a rapidly evolving restaurant culture blending traditional and modern
  • Nature — the city is 50% green space, and the Neris River runs through the centre
  • Vibe — relaxed, creative, and increasingly cosmopolitan

Did You Know?

Vilnius has more baroque architecture than any other city in Northern Europe, earning it the nickname "The Rome of the North".

docs.title: Top 5 Must-See Spots
docs.text: # Attraction District Why Visit 1 Gediminas Tower Old Town Panoramic city views, symbol of Vilnius 2 Vilnius Cathedral Old Town Stunning neoclassical architecture 3 Užupis Republic Užupis Quirky artist district with its own constitution 4 Gate of Dawn Old Town Most important religious site in the city 5 Bernardine Garden Old Town Beautiful riverside park below the castle hill

docs.title: Repository Structure Proposal — Vilnius Travel Guide
Based on the Customer Journey Map (CJM) Approach
Why Structure Before More Content
docs.text:
The existing site has solid content, but its navigation is organised around topics (Guide / Districts / Food & Drink). This is a common starting point, but it forces the user to self-orient — they have to figure out where they are in their own journey and find the right page themselves.

A CJM-based structure reorganises around the traveller's mental state at each stage, not around the content categories we happen to have. Pages are ordered by urgency, existing knowledge, stress levels, and next steps.

docs.title: The Traveller's Journey — Five Stages
docs.text: Stage User's mindset Primary question Reading behaviour 1 · Deciding Curious, uncommitted "Is Vilnius worth it?" Scanning, skimming 2 · Planning Motivated, needs facts "What do I need to arrange?" Reading carefully 3 · Getting There Slightly stressed "Am I doing this right?" Quick reference 4 · Exploring Present, time-limited "What should I do next?" Short bursts 5 · Going Deeper Comfortable, curious "What am I missing?" Browsing freely

docs.title: Proposed Repository Structure
docs.text:
vilnius-guide/
│
├── docs/
│   │
│   ├── index.md                    ← Hero: Why Vilnius? (Stage 1)
│   │
│   ├── 01-deciding/                ← STAGE 1 · Deciding
│   │   ├── why-vilnius.md          ← "Is it worth the trip?" — the pitch
│   │   ├── best-time.md            ← When to go (already exists, moved here)
│   │   └── how-long.md             ← 2 days vs 4 days: what you can cover
│   │
│   ├── 02-planning/                ← STAGE 2 · Planning
│   │   ├── getting-started.md      ← Visas, entry, currency (already exists)
│   │   ├── budgeting.md            ← Day-by-day cost breakdowns
│   │   ├── where-to-stay.md        ← Neighbourhood guide for accommodation
│   │   └── itineraries/
│   │       ├── 2-day.md            ← Quick visit itinerary
│   │       ├── 4-day.md            ← Full first-visit itinerary
│   │       └── with-kids.md        ← Family-specific itinerary
│   │
│   ├── 03-arriving/                ← STAGE 3 · Getting There & Arriving
│   │   ├── getting-around.md       ← Airport → city, transport (already exists)
│   │   ├── sim-and-connectivity.md ← SIM cards, Wi-Fi, apps (split from getting-started)
│   │   └── safety-and-tips.md      ← What to know on day one
│   │
│   ├── 04-exploring/               ← STAGE 4 · Exploring the City
│   │   ├── districts/
│   │   │   ├── old-town.md         ← (already exists)
│   │   │   ├── uzupis.md           ← (already exists)
│   │   │   └── shnipishkes.md      ← (already exists)
│   │   ├── sights/
│   │   │   ├── top-10.md           ← NEW: scannable "don't miss" reference
│   │   │   └── free-sights.md      ← NEW: budget traveller reference
│   │   ├── food/
│   │   │   ├── cuisine.md          ← (already exists)
│   │   │   ├── restaurants.md      ← (already exists)
│   │   │   └── cafes.md            ← (already exists)
│   │   └── day-trips/
│   │       └── trakai.md           ← NEW: Trakai day trip guide
│   │
│   ├── 05-deeper/                  ← STAGE 5 · Going Deeper
│   │   ├── history.md              ← For those who want context
│   │   ├── language.md             ← More phrases, culture notes
│   │   └── local-life.md           ← Markets, events, living like a local
│   │
│   ├── reference/                  ← Quick-lookup reference (any stage)
│   │   ├── emergency-contacts.md
│   │   ├── useful-apps.md
│   │   └── phrasebook.md
│   │
│   └── about.md                    ← (already exists)
│
├── mkdocs.yml
├── README.md
└── .gitignore

docs.title: How Each CJM Principle Maps to the Structure
1 · Pages ordered by urgency and stress level
docs.text: The 01-deciding → 02-planning → 03-arriving → 04-exploring → 05-deeper flow mirrors the real emotional arc of a trip. A traveller arriving at the airport does not want the "Why Vilnius?" page — they want getting-around.md. The numbered folders make this explicit to both users and contributors.

docs.title: 2 · Prerequisites before steps (no hidden blockers)
docs.text: The existing getting-started.md mixes visa entry (a blocker, Stage 2) with SIM cards (practical on arrival, Stage 3) and safety tips (Stage 3). The new structure separates these: blockers live in 02-planning/, day-one practical info lives in 03-arriving/. This respects the user's time and prevents the frustration of finding critical information buried mid-page.

docs.title: 3 · One document type per page
docs.text:
The CJM material warns against "a single page trying to serve multiple purposes." The proposed structure creates clear genre assignments:

Page Document type Why why-vilnius.md Pitch / overview Scanning reader, uncommitted getting-started.md Getting-started guide Motivated reader, needs checklist getting-around.md How-to Stressed reader, wants steps top-10.md Reference Time-pressured reader, wants quick answers history.md Tutorial/explainer Comfortable reader, wants depth

docs.title: 4 · Clear next steps at every stage
docs.text: Each section folder ends with a natural handoff. A traveller who finishes 02-planning/ is directed to 03-arriving/; someone exploring 04-exploring/districts/ is prompted to check 04-exploring/food/. This creates the coherent documentation journey the CJM calls for — individual pages are good, but the overall set must connect.

docs.title: 5 · A reference/ section for any-stage lookups
docs.text: Reference pages behave differently from journey pages: users jump in from anywhere, scan fast, and leave. Keeping them in a dedicated reference/ folder prevents them from cluttering the journey flow while making them immediately findable via search.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The core shift is this: the existing structure asks the user "what topic are you interested in?" The CJM structure asks "where are you in your journey, and what do you need right now?"

That single reframe changes how pages are ordered, how they are named, what they include, and how they hand off to each other — which is precisely what the CJM approach is designed to achieve.

docs.title: About This Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the Vilnius Travel Guide — an open, community-friendly documentation site built to help travellers explore one of Europe's most underrated capital cities.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This guide aims to be:

  • Practical — real information you can use on the ground
  • Up to date — regularly reviewed and updated
  • Honest — genuine recommendations, not paid placements
  • Accessible — written in plain English for international visitors

docs.title: About Vilnius
docs.text:
Vilnius is the capital of Lithuania, a country of 2.8 million people on the eastern shore of the Baltic Sea. Lithuania was the last country in Europe to officially adopt Christianity (1387), and its capital reflects this unique history — a city where pagan traditions, Catholic baroque, Jewish heritage, Soviet modernism, and contemporary European culture all exist side by side.

Fact Detail Country Lithuania Region Baltic States, Northern Europe EU Member Since 2004 NATO Member Since 2004 Population (city) ~580,000 Area 401 km² Elevation 112 m above sea level Founded ~1323 (by Grand Duke Gediminas) UNESCO status Old Town listed since 1994

docs.title: A Brief History
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[1323 - Founded by Gediminas] --> B[1387 - Capital of Grand Duchy of Lithuania]
    B --> C[1795 - Russian Empire annexation]
    C --> D[1918 - Independence declared]
    D --> E[1940 - Soviet occupation]
    E --> F[1990 - Independence restored]
    F --> G[2004 - EU and NATO membership]
    G --> H[Today - Vibrant European capital]

docs.title: How to Contribute
docs.text:
This site is built with MkDocs and the Material theme, with content written in Markdown. All source files are hosted on GitHub.

If you notice outdated information, incorrect prices, or want to suggest a new restaurant or attraction, contributions are welcome.

docs.title: Reporting Issues
docs.text:
• Open an issue on the GitHub repository
  • Describe what is incorrect or missing
  • Include the page name and section

docs.title: Submitting Changes
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository
  2. Create a branch: git checkout -b fix/page-name
  3. Edit the relevant .md file in the docs/ folder
  4. Submit a pull request with a clear description

docs.title: Built With
docs.text:
Tool Purpose MkDocs Static site generator Material for MkDocs Theme GitHub Pages Hosting Markdown Content format

Disclaimer

All prices, hours, and practical information in this guide are approximate and subject to change. Always verify details directly with venues before visiting.

docs.title: Best Time to Visit
docs.text: Vilnius has four distinct seasons, each offering a different experience of the city.

docs.title: Season by Season
docs.text:
☀️ Summer (Jun–Aug)🍂 Autumn (Sep–Nov)❄️ Winter (Dec–Feb)🌸 Spring (Mar–May)

The peak season. Long days (up to 17 hours of daylight), outdoor terraces packed, festivals in full swing.

  • Average temperature: 18–24°C
  • Crowds: High
  • Prices: Highest
  • Highlights: Vilnius Street Music Day (June), outdoor cinema, beer gardens

Best for

First-time visitors, outdoor lovers, festival-goers.

A hidden favourite. The city turns gold and amber, crowds thin out, and prices drop. September is particularly lovely.

  • Average temperature: 5–15°C
  • Crowds: Low–Medium
  • Prices: Lower
  • Highlights: Vilnius City Days (September), mushroom season, cosy cafes

Local Tip

September is arguably the best month — summer warmth lingers, the Old Town is beautiful in early autumn colour, and tourists are gone.

A fairy-tale atmosphere. The Christmas market in Cathedral Square is magical. Cold, but rarely extreme.

  • Average temperature: -5 to +2°C
  • Crowds: Low (except Christmas/New Year)
  • Prices: Lowest
  • Highlights: Christmas Market (Dec), ice skating, cosy restaurant season

Expect

Short days (as few as 7.5 hours of daylight), possible snow and ice on cobblestones — pack warm layers and good boots.

The city wakes up. Trees blossom, terraces reopen, and the city feels energetic. May is excellent.

  • Average temperature: 5–15°C
  • Crowds: Low–Medium
  • Prices: Mid-range
  • Highlights: Easter celebrations, Kaziukas Fair (March), Botanical Garden bloom

docs.title: Average Weather by Month
docs.text: Month Avg High (°C) Avg Low (°C) Rain Days Notes January 0 -6 10 Coldest month, possible snow February 1 -6 9 Still cold, days getting longer March 7 -1 10 Kaziukas craft fair April 13 4 11 Warming up, cherry blossoms May 19 8 12 Excellent — warm and green June 22 12 13 Peak season begins July 24 14 13 Hottest month August 23 13 12 Still summer, slightly quieter September 18 9 12 Best month overall October 11 4 12 Autumn colours peak November 4 0 13 Grey, but atmospheric December 1 -4 12 Christmas market magic

docs.title: How Long to Stay
docs.text: Vilnius is compact enough to see the highlights in 2 days, but rewarding enough that 4–5 days never feels wasted.

docs.title: Quick Guide
docs.text: Duration What you can cover Best for 2 days Old Town, Užupis, key sights, one good meal Weekend trip 3 days Everything above + day trip to Trakai First visit 4–5 days Full exploration, Šnipiškės, slower pace Deeper visit 7+ days All of the above + Kaunas, Curonian Spit Extended stay

docs.title: 2 Days — The Essential Visit
docs.text:
You can see the core of Vilnius well in two full days. This works well as a weekend trip.

Day 1 — Old Town Morning at Vilnius Cathedral and Gediminas Tower, afternoon wandering Pilies Street and the hidden courtyards, evening dinner in the Old Town.

Day 2 — Užupis and beyond Morning in Užupis, afternoon at the Gate of Dawn and St Anne's Church, evening at a rooftop bar in Šnipiškės for the skyline view.

docs.title: 3 Days — The Recommended Visit
docs.text:
Three days gives you breathing room and space for a day trip.

Day 1 & 2 — as above Day 3 — Day trip to Trakai Castle (45 min by train), back in the evening for a final dinner in the Old Town.

docs.title: 4–5 Days — The Full Picture
docs.text:
With more time you can slow down, explore the food scene properly, visit the Museum of Occupations and Struggles, and wander the Bernardine Garden at leisure.

Our recommendation

3 days is the sweet spot for a first visit. Long enough to feel the city properly, short enough to stay focused.

docs.title: Why Vilnius?
docs.text: Vilnius is one of Europe's best-kept secrets — a medieval capital that offers the beauty of Prague or Tallinn at a fraction of the price, without the crowds.

docs.title: It's genuinely beautiful
docs.text: The Old Town is one of the largest and best-preserved in all of Europe — a UNESCO World Heritage Site packed with Gothic churches, Baroque courtyards, and hidden passages. You can walk for hours and keep finding new corners.

docs.title: It's affordable
docs.text: Compared to Western European capitals, Vilnius is remarkably cheap. A excellent restaurant dinner costs €15–25. A coffee is €2–3. A hotel in the centre starts from €50. Your money goes far here.

docs.title: It's compact and easy
docs.text: The entire Old Town is walkable. The airport is 7 minutes by train from the centre. English is spoken everywhere. There is no steep learning curve.

docs.title: It has a distinct personality
docs.text: Vilnius is not trying to be Paris or Berlin. It has its own character — shaped by Lithuanian, Polish, Jewish, Russian, and Soviet history all layered on top of each other. The result is genuinely unique.

docs.title: Užupis alone is worth it
docs.text: An entire neighbourhood that declared itself an independent republic in 1997, complete with its own constitution, president, and army of 12 people. Nowhere else in Europe has anything quite like it.

docs.title: Who Is Vilnius For?
docs.text: Traveller type Why Vilnius works History lovers 700 years of layered history in one walkable city Food travellers Rapidly evolving restaurant scene, excellent coffee culture Budget travellers Comparable beauty to Prague at significantly lower cost Weekend trippers Compact enough to see well in 2–3 days Off-the-beaten-path seekers Still genuinely undiscovered by mass tourism

docs.title: Common Concerns
docs.text:
"Isn't Lithuania far away?" Vilnius has direct flights from most major European cities. From London, Berlin, or Warsaw it is a 2–3 hour flight. Ryanair, Wizz Air, and airBaltic all serve the route cheaply.

"Will I understand anything?" English is widely spoken, especially by anyone under 40. Menus, signs in tourist areas, and transport apps are all available in English.

"Is it safe?" Vilnius consistently ranks among the safest capitals in Europe. Violent crime against tourists is extremely rare.

Still not sure?

September is the single best month to visit — warm weather, autumn colours beginning, no summer crowds, and lower prices. If you can only go once, go in September.

docs.title: Budgeting
docs.text: Vilnius is one of the most affordable capitals in the European Union. Here is what to realistically expect to spend.

docs.title: Daily Budget by Level
docs.text: Level Per day Includes Budget €40–60 Hostel dorm (€15–20), supermarket meals or cheap eats (€15), free sights, public transport Mid-range €80–130 3-star hotel (€50–80), restaurant lunch and dinner (€25–40), one paid attraction Comfort €150–250+ Boutique hotel (€100–150), fine dining (€50–80), guided tours

docs.title: Accommodation
docs.text: Type Price per night Hostel dorm €15–25 Budget hotel €40–60 3-star hotel €60–100 Boutique hotel €100–180 Apartment (Airbnb) €50–90

docs.title: Food & Drink
docs.text: Item Price Coffee (espresso/flat white) €2–3 Beer in a bar €3–5 Lunch in a café €8–12 Dinner in a mid-range restaurant €15–25 per person Fine dining tasting menu €50–80 per person Supermarket meal deal €4–6 Cepelinai (national dish) €8–12

docs.title: Transport
docs.text: Journey Price Airport train to centre €0.65 Single bus/trolleybus ticket €1.00 Day transport pass €3.00 Bolt ride across the city €4–8 Bolt scooter (short trip) €1–2

docs.title: Attractions
docs.text: Attraction Entry Gediminas Tower €5 adults / €2.50 students Most churches (Cathedral, Gate of Dawn) Free Museum of Occupations €5 Trakai Castle €12 Europa Tower observation deck €5

docs.title: Money-Saving Tips
docs.text:
• Eat lunch out, cook or supermarket for dinner — lunch menus (set lunches) are common and excellent value at €7–10.
  • Use the M-Ticket app for public transport — saves 35% vs cash fares.
  • Visit churches for free — most of the most beautiful buildings in Vilnius have no entry charge.
  • Bolt over taxis — always cheaper and more reliable than street taxis.
  • Stay just outside the Old Town — 10 minutes walk saves 30–40% on accommodation.

ATMs

Use bank ATMs — Swedbank, SEB, and Luminor have branches throughout the city. Avoid standalone ATMs in tourist areas which charge high fees. Always choose to be charged in Euros, not your home currency.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This page covers everything you need to know before your trip to Vilnius — visas, money, language, and practical essentials.

docs.title: Entry Requirements
docs.text:
EU / EEA Citizens

No visa required. A valid national ID card or passport is sufficient. Lithuania is part of the Schengen Area.

US / UK / CA / AU Citizens

No visa required for stays up to 90 days within any 180-day period. A valid passport is required.

Other Countries

Check the Lithuanian Ministry of Foreign Affairs website for visa requirements specific to your nationality.

Passport Validity

Your passport should be valid for at least 3 months beyond your planned departure date from the Schengen Area.

docs.title: Money & Budget
docs.text:
Lithuania uses the Euro (€). Vilnius is affordable compared to most Western European capitals.

Budget Level Daily Spend (approx.) What You Get Budget €40–60 Hostel dorm, cheap eats, free sights Mid-range €80–130 3-star hotel, restaurant meals, paid attractions Comfort €150–250+ Boutique hotel, fine dining, guided tours

ATMs

ATMs are widely available throughout the city. Use bank ATMs (Swedbank, SEB, Luminor) to avoid high fees from independent ATM operators. Most restaurants and shops accept card payments.

docs.title: Language
docs.text:
The official language is Lithuanian, one of the oldest living Indo-European languages. However, English is widely spoken, especially by younger people and in tourist areas.

Useful Lithuanian phrases:

Lithuanian Pronunciation English Labas lah-bahs Hello Ačiū ah-choo Thank you Prašom prah-shom Please / You're welcome Atsiprašau aht-see-prah-show Excuse me / Sorry Kiek kainuoja? kyek kai-nuo-yah How much does it cost?

docs.title: Where to Stay
docs.text: Vilnius is compact enough that location matters less than in larger cities. But the neighbourhood you choose does shape your experience.

docs.title: Old Town (Senamiestis) — Best for first-timers
docs.text:
The heart of Vilnius. Staying here means everything is walkable — churches, restaurants, cafes, and sights are all within minutes.

  • Best for: First visit, couples, anyone who wants to walk everywhere
  • Price: Highest — but still affordable by European standards
  • Vibe: Historic, lively in the evenings, some noise on weekends

docs.title: Užupis — Best for atmosphere
docs.text:
The bohemian republic-within-a-city. Quiet, artistic, and charming. A 10-minute walk from Cathedral Square.

  • Best for: Repeat visitors, creative travellers, those who want a quieter base
  • Price: Slightly lower than Old Town
  • Vibe: Calm, artsy, riverside

docs.title: New Town (Naujamiestis) — Best for budget
docs.text:
Just west of the Old Town. More residential, with good transport links and lower prices. Gedimino Avenue is the main commercial street.

  • Best for: Budget travellers, longer stays, business visitors
  • Price: Lower
  • Vibe: Local, everyday, less touristy

docs.title: Šnipiškės (CBD) — Best for views
docs.text:
The modern glass district across the river. Great skyline views of the Old Town. A 15-minute walk or one bus stop from the centre.

  • Best for: Those who want modern hotels, business travellers
  • Price: Mid to high
  • Vibe: Contemporary, quiet at night

docs.title: Our Recommendation
docs.text:
Stay in the Old Town or Užupis for your first visit. The walkability is genuinely valuable — you will wander back to your accommodation between sights, rest, and head out again. This is much harder if you are a 20-minute commute away.

Booking advice

Book at least 2–3 weeks ahead for summer (June–August) and around Christmas. September and October have good availability. Use Booking.com — it has the widest selection for Vilnius.

docs.title: 2-Day Itinerary
docs.text: A focused two-day visit covering the essential Vilnius. Works perfectly as a weekend trip.

docs.title: Morning
docs.text:
Start at Cathedral Square (09:00). This is the symbolic heart of Vilnius. Look for the Stebuklas tile — stand on it and make a wish.

Walk up to Gediminas Tower (allow 1 hour including the climb). The views over the Old Town and the Neris River are the best in the city. Go early to avoid queues.

docs.title: Late Morning
docs.text: Descend and walk south along Pilies Street — the main medieval street of the Old Town. Browse the amber shops and stop for a coffee at Crooked Nose & Coffee Stories (Pilies g. 22).

docs.title: Lunch
docs.text: Etno Dvaras (Pilies g. 16) for traditional Lithuanian food. Order the cepelinai — the national dish. Budget €12–15.

docs.title: Afternoon
docs.text:
Explore the hidden courtyards off Pilies Street and the surrounding streets. Walk down to the Gate of Dawn — the only surviving city gate, with a pilgrimage chapel above it.

Visit St Anne's Church — one of the most beautiful Gothic buildings in Northern Europe. Napoleon reportedly wanted to carry it back to Paris.

docs.title: Evening
docs.text: Dinner in the Old Town. Try Lokys (Stikliu g. 8) for game dishes in a Gothic cellar, or keep it simpler at any of the terraces around Town Hall Square.

docs.title: Morning
docs.text:
Walk to Užupis (10 minutes from the Old Town). Read the Constitution plaques on Paupio Street. Find the Angel of Užupis at the main intersection.

Browse the galleries and studios — many are open from 10:00. Coffee at Užupio Kavinė by the river if the weather is good.

docs.title: Lunch
docs.text: Senoji Kibininė (Bernardinu g. 9) for kibinai — flaky meat pastries that are the perfect quick lunch. Budget €6–8.

docs.title: Afternoon
docs.text:
Cross the Mindaugas Bridge into Šnipiškės. Walk the riverbank promenade for the best views of the Old Town skyline — particularly good at this time of day.

Optional: take the lift to the Europa Tower observation deck (€5) for 360° city views.

docs.title: Evening
docs.text:
Sunset drink at Skybar (22nd floor, Šnipiškės) — the view of the Cathedral lit up across the river is one of Vilnius's finest sights. Then walk back over the bridge to the Old Town for a final dinner.

Getting around

You will not need public transport for this itinerary — everything is walkable. Wear comfortable shoes. The cobblestones in the Old Town are uneven.

docs.title: Getting Around
docs.text: Vilnius is a compact city and very easy to navigate. The Old Town is almost entirely walkable, and public transport covers the rest efficiently.

docs.title: From the Airport
docs.text:
Vilnius Airport (VNO) is located just 7 km south of the city centre — one of the most conveniently located airports in Europe.

Option Duration Cost Notes Train 7 min €0.65 Runs every 30 min, most convenient Bus (No. 1, 2) 30–40 min €1 Slower but runs more frequently Bolt / Uber 15–20 min €8–12 Door to door, best late at night Taxi 15–20 min €15–25 Use only licensed taxis

Best Option

The train is the fastest and cheapest option. Buy tickets at the station kiosk or via the Trafi app.

docs.title: Walking
docs.text: The Old Town is best explored on foot. Most major sights are within a 20-minute walk of Cathedral Square. Wear comfortable shoes — the cobblestones can be uneven.

docs.title: Public Transport
docs.text:
Vilnius has an extensive bus and trolleybus network.

  • Fare: €1.00 (cash on board) or €0.65 (with an M-Ticket card)
  • Day pass: €3.00
  • Operating hours: ~05:30–23:00 (night buses on weekends)
  • App: Download Trafi for real-time routes and schedules

docs.title: Bolt Scooters
docs.text:
Electric scooters are available throughout the city via the Bolt app. A fun and cheap way to cover medium distances.

  • Unlock fee: €0.50
  • Per minute: €0.15

docs.title: CycloCity Bike Share
docs.text:
Vilnius has a well-developed cycling infrastructure.

  • Available at 60+ stations across the city
  • First 30 minutes free with a day pass (€2)
  • Great for riverside routes along the Neris

docs.title: Car Rental & Taxis
docs.text:
Driving in Old Town

Most of the Old Town is a restricted traffic zone. You need a permit to drive there. For visiting the Old Town, always use public transport or walk.

  • Licensed taxis are metered. Starting fare ~€1.30, ~€0.80/km
  • Always use Bolt or Uber apps to avoid overcharging

docs.title: Key Routes
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Airport VNO] -->|Train 7 min| B[Vilnius Train Station]
    B -->|Walk 15 min| C[Cathedral Square]
    C -->|Walk 10 min| D[Gediminas Tower]
    C -->|Walk 5 min| E[Old Town Streets]
    C -->|Walk 20 min| F[Uzupis]
    B -->|Bus 15 min| G[Shnipishkes CBD]

docs.title: Safety & Tips
docs.text: Vilnius is a very safe city for tourists. Violent crime is rare.

docs.title: Safe Practices
docs.text:
• Keep an eye on your belongings in crowded areas
  • Use licensed taxis or Bolt/Uber apps
  • Avoid poorly lit streets in unfamiliar areas late at night

docs.title: Useful Apps
docs.text:
• Bolt — the dominant ride-hailing app (cheaper than taxis)
  • Trafi — public transport routes and real-time schedules
  • Google Maps — works excellently for navigation in Vilnius
  • Vilnius – the city in your pocket — official city guide app

docs.title: SIM Cards
docs.text: Available at the airport and convenience stores (Tele2, Bite, Telia). A prepaid SIM with 10GB data costs around €5–10.

docs.title: Wi-Fi
docs.text: Free Wi-Fi is available in most cafes, restaurants, hotels, and public spaces including the Old Town.

docs.title: Old Town (Senamiestis)
docs.text:
Vilnius Old Town is the historic heart of the city and one of the largest and best-preserved medieval old towns in Europe, designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994.

Area Facts

  • Size: 3.6 km² — the largest Old Town in the Baltic states
  • UNESCO status: World Heritage Site since 1994
  • Architecture: Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque, Classicism — all in one walk

docs.title: Vilnius Cathedral & Bell Tower
docs.text:
The Cathedral of St Stanislaus and St Vladislaus is the main Roman Catholic cathedral of Lithuania. The neoclassical building dominates Cathedral Square — the symbolic centre of the city.

  • Hours: Mon–Sat 07:00–19:00, Sun 07:00–20:00
  • Entry: Free
  • Tip: Look for the Stebuklas (Miracle) tile in the square — it marks the starting point of the Baltic Way, the 1989 human chain across all three Baltic states.

docs.title: Gediminas Tower
docs.text:
The only remaining part of the Upper Castle, perched on a hill above the Old Town. Offers the best panoramic views of Vilnius.

  • Hours: Daily 10:00–19:00 (summer), 10:00–17:00 (winter)
  • Entry: €5 adults, €2.50 students
  • Access: Walk up (15 min) or take the funicular railway

docs.title: Gate of Dawn (Ausros Vartai)
docs.text:
The only remaining gate of the old city wall. Inside is a small chapel housing a famous image of the Virgin Mary — one of the most important pilgrimage sites in Central and Eastern Europe.

  • Hours: Daily 06:00–19:00
  • Entry: Free
  • Note: Dress modestly when entering the chapel.

docs.title: St Anne's Church
docs.text:
A stunning example of Flamboyant Gothic architecture, reportedly so admired by Napoleon that he wished he could carry it back to Paris in the palm of his hand.

  • Hours: Tue–Sat 10:00–18:00, Sun 10:00–15:00
  • Entry: Free

docs.title: Recommended Walking Route
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Cathedral Square] --> B[Pilies Street]
    B --> C[St Annes Church]
    C --> D[Bernardine Garden]
    D --> E[Uzupis Bridge]
    E --> F[Maironio Street back]
    F --> A

Allow 2–3 hours for a leisurely walk with stops.

docs.title: Tips for Visiting
docs.text:
Do's

  • Explore the courtyards hidden behind the main streets — some of the best surprises are off the beaten path
  • Visit early morning (before 09:00) for atmospheric, crowd-free streets
  • Walk up to Gediminas Tower at sunset for golden-hour views

Don'ts

  • Don't rely on a car — most of the Old Town is pedestrianised
  • Don't skip the side streets — Stikliu, Sv. Mykolo, and Bernardinu are all beautiful

docs.title: Šnipiškės (New City Centre)
docs.text:
Šnipiškės, located just across the Neris River from the Old Town, is Vilnius's modern business district — a striking contrast of glass skyscrapers, contemporary architecture, and riverside promenades.

Quick Facts

  • Location: North bank of the Neris River, opposite the Old Town
  • Character: Business, modern, cosmopolitan
  • Best for: Skyline views, modern restaurants, rooftop bars

docs.title: Why Visit
docs.text: While most tourists stay in the Old Town, Šnipiškės is worth crossing the river for. From here you get the best views of Vilnius Cathedral and the Old Town skyline, especially at sunset. The district has also developed a strong restaurant and bar scene in recent years.

docs.title: Vilnius Skyline Viewpoint
docs.text: The riverbank promenade on the north side of the Neris offers a perfect view of the Old Town and Cathedral across the water. Particularly stunning at golden hour.

docs.title: Europa Tower
docs.text:
At 148 metres, it was the tallest building in the Baltic states when completed in 2004. The observation deck on the 26th floor offers 360° views of the city.

  • Hours: Mon–Fri 09:00–18:00
  • Entry: €5

docs.title: Panorama Shopping Centre
docs.text: The largest shopping mall in Lithuania, with over 200 shops, a cinema, and dining options. Useful for rainy days.

docs.title: Restaurants & Bars
docs.text:
Šnipiškės has developed into one of the best areas for contemporary dining in Vilnius.

Venue Type Notes Apvalus Stulas Fine dining Tasting menus, panoramic views Stekai Steakhouse Best steaks in Vilnius Skybar Rooftop bar 22nd floor, city views Laumės Juosta Casual Good brunch spot

docs.title: Getting There
docs.text:
From the Old Town, cross the Mindaugas Bridge (a 10-minute walk from Cathedral Square) or take any bus heading north across the Neris.

Evening Visit

Come at sunset and walk back across the bridge to the Old Town — the view of the lit-up Cathedral and tower is one of Vilnius's best sights.

docs.title: Užupis
docs.text:
Užupis (meaning "beyond the river" in Lithuanian) is Vilnius's most eccentric and beloved neighbourhood. On April 1, 1997, it declared itself an independent republic — complete with its own president, army (of about 12 people), currency, and a constitution.

The Republic of Užupis

  • Declared independent: April 1, 1997
  • Population: ~7,000
  • President: Romas Lileikis (artist and filmmaker)
  • National holiday: April 1 (April Fools' Day)
  • Constitution: Posted on the street in 23 languages

docs.title: The Constitution
docs.text:
The Užupis Constitution is one of the neighbourhood's great delights. It is engraved on mirrored plaques on Paupio Street in dozens of languages. Some memorable articles:

Article 1: Everyone has the right to live by the River Vilnelė, and the River Vilnelė has the right to flow by everyone.

Article 12: Everyone has the right to be idle.

Article 41: Everyone has the right to be happy.

Article 42: Everyone is not obliged to be happy.

Find the plaques

Walk along Paupio Street beside the small Vilnelė river to read the full constitution. It's one of the most charming walks in Vilnius.

docs.title: Angel of Užupis
docs.text: The neighbourhood's symbol — a bronze trumpet-playing angel on a column at the main intersection. Cast in 2002, it represents freedom and the creative spirit of the area.

docs.title: Galleries & Studios
docs.text:
Užupis is packed with independent art galleries, artist studios, and craft workshops. Many are open to visitors. Look out for:

  • The Užupis Art Incubator
  • Small photography galleries on the back streets
  • Ceramic and textile workshops

docs.title: Cafes & Bars
docs.text: The neighbourhood has a relaxed, bohemian cafe culture. Grab a coffee, sit by the river, and watch the world go by.

docs.title: Crossing the Border
docs.text:
On April 1st (the Republic's national holiday), the Užupis bridges become "border crossings" where guards stamp your passport with the Užupis stamp. Highly recommended if you happen to be in Vilnius on that date.

"Border" Bridge Location Užupio tiltas Main entry from Old Town Malūnų tiltas Southern entry

docs.title: Getting There
docs.text:
Užupis is a 10-minute walk from Cathedral Square. Walk east through the Old Town along Pilies Street, then turn left past St Anne's Church and cross the small Vilnelė river.

Best Time to Visit

Late afternoon on a weekday — the light on the river is beautiful, the streets are quiet, and you can browse the galleries without crowds.

docs.title: Cafes & Coffee
docs.text:
Vilnius has a thriving, serious coffee culture. The city is packed with excellent independent specialty cafes, and the standard of espresso-based drinks is genuinely high. You will not struggle to find a great cup of coffee here.

Coffee Culture

Vilnius baristas regularly compete and win at European-level barista championships. The city takes its coffee seriously.

docs.title: Crooked Nose & Coffee Stories
docs.text:
The flagship of Vilnius's specialty coffee scene. Multiple locations, consistently excellent espresso and filter coffee. The Old Town branch is a great place to start the day.

  • Best for: Espresso, pour-over, single origins
  • Location: Pilies g. 22 (Old Town) + other branches
  • Vibe: Modern, friendly, knowledgeable staff
  • Price: €€

docs.title: Caffeine
docs.text:
A beloved local chain that helped establish the specialty coffee movement in Vilnius. Excellent beans, beautiful spaces, and very good food menus.

  • Best for: Flat whites, brunch, working remotely
  • Location: Multiple locations across the city
  • Vibe: Relaxed, good for laptops, always busy
  • Price: €€

docs.title: Finger's Coffee
docs.text:
Small, intimate specialty cafe near the Old Town. Takes its single-origin filter coffee very seriously.

  • Best for: Filter coffee, quiet mornings
  • Location: Saviciaus g. 10
  • Vibe: Quiet, focused, coffee-forward
  • Price: €€

docs.title: Kava Kahve Cafe
docs.text:
A cosy, eclectic cafe in the Old Town with one of the longest coffee menus in the city. Also serves good light food.

  • Best for: Variety, atmosphere, people-watching
  • Location: Sv. Kazimiero g. 3, Old Town
  • Vibe: Warm, eclectic, characterful
  • Price: €

docs.title: Cafes with a View or Atmosphere
Šv. Kazimiero Kavinė
docs.text: Set in a beautiful courtyard of the Church of St Casimir. A peaceful spot for coffee and cake away from the main tourist streets.

docs.title: Užupio Kavinė
docs.text:
Perched right on the Vilnelė riverbank in Užupis. Perfect in summer — sit outside and watch the river while drinking coffee or local beer.

  • Location: Užupio g. 2
  • Best in: Summer, sunny days
  • Vibe: Bohemian, relaxed, artistic crowd

docs.title: What to Order
docs.text:
Espresso-basedFilter / Pour-overCold DrinksWith Food

Lithuanian cafes follow Italian espresso traditions. Order a flat white, cortado, or cappuccino for the best expression of the coffee. Milk quality is generally very high.

Specialty cafes all offer filter options. Ask for the single origin of the day at Crooked Nose or Finger's — staff will happily explain the origin and tasting notes.

Cold brew is widely available in summer. Matcha lattes have become very popular in Vilnius cafes. Freshly squeezed juices are also common.

Most Vilnius cafes serve excellent food alongside coffee — particularly good for breakfast and brunch. Look for ryžiai su daržovėmis (rice with vegetables) or locally baked pastries.

docs.title: Cafe Hours & Tips
docs.text:
Opening Hours

Most independent cafes open around 08:00–09:00 and close between 20:00–22:00. Sunday hours are often shorter. Always check Google Maps for current hours.

Remote Working

Vilnius cafes are very laptop-friendly. Caffeine and Crooked Nose both have good Wi-Fi and plenty of seating. Weekday mornings are the quietest time to work.

docs.title: Lithuanian Cuisine
docs.text:
Lithuanian cuisine is hearty, honest, and deeply tied to the country's agricultural traditions. Shaped by cold winters, forest foraging, and centuries of peasant cooking, it centers on potatoes, rye bread, dairy, pork, and wild mushrooms.

Key Ingredients

  • Potatoes — the cornerstone of Lithuanian cooking
  • Rye bread (ruginė duona) — dark, dense, and delicious
  • Dairy — sour cream (grietinė) appears on almost everything
  • Pork — the most common meat
  • Wild mushrooms & berries — foraged from Lithuanian forests

docs.title: Cepelinai (Zeppelins)
docs.text:
The national dish. Large dumplings made from grated and mashed potatoes, stuffed with minced pork and served with sour cream and bacon bits. Named after the Zeppelin airship due to their shape.

Where to try it

Almost every traditional Lithuanian restaurant serves cepelinai. Try Etno Dvaras in the Old Town for a classic version.

docs.title: Šaltibarščiai (Cold Beet Soup)
docs.text:
A vibrant pink cold soup made from kefir, beetroot, cucumber, and dill. A beloved summer dish, always served with hot boiled potatoes on the side.

  • Best enjoyed: May–September
  • Colour: Unmistakably pink — you will recognise it immediately

docs.title: Kibinai
docs.text:
Pastry pockets filled with meat (usually lamb or pork), originally from the Karaite minority of Trakai. Crispy, buttery, and deeply satisfying.

Day Trip Connection

Kibinai are strongly associated with Trakai (45 min from Vilnius) — a highly recommended day trip where you can eat kibinai at the lakeside.

docs.title: Juoda duona (Dark Rye Bread)
docs.text: Lithuanian rye bread is unlike anything you'll find in Western Europe — dense, slightly sour, and intensely flavoured. Often served with butter, lard, or garlic.

docs.title: Skilandis
docs.text: A traditional smoked sausage made from minced pork and spices, cold-smoked and dried. A staple of Lithuanian charcuterie boards.

docs.title: Kugelis
docs.text: A baked potato pudding — dense, savoury, and filling. Made from grated potatoes, eggs, onions, and bacon. Comfort food at its finest.

docs.title: Beer (Alus)
docs.text: Lithuania has a strong craft beer culture. Local breweries like Vilkmergės, Gubernija, and Dundulis produce excellent beers. The Old Town has numerous craft beer bars.

docs.title: Midus (Mead)
docs.text: Traditional Lithuanian honey wine. Slightly sweet, mildly alcoholic, and very much worth trying. Available at most traditional restaurants.

docs.title: Kefir (Kefyras)
docs.text: Lithuanians drink a lot of kefir — a fermented milk drink. Pick some up at a supermarket; it is cheap, healthy, and a genuine part of local daily life.

docs.title: Dietary Notes
docs.text:
VegetariansVegansGluten-free

Lithuanian cuisine is traditionally meat-heavy, but Vilnius has a growing vegetarian and vegan restaurant scene. Most modern restaurants offer vegetarian options. Šaltibarščiai (cold beet soup) is naturally vegetarian.

Dedicated vegan restaurants exist in Vilnius (try Radharane or Vero Cafe). Traditional cuisine is harder to navigate — look for mushroom dishes, salads, and rye bread sides.

Challenging in traditional restaurants as rye bread and potato dumplings are staples. Modern restaurants increasingly offer gluten-free options — always ask staff directly.

docs.title: Best Restaurants
docs.text:
Vilnius has a rapidly evolving restaurant scene that punches well above its size. From traditional Lithuanian cuisine to creative modern cooking, the city offers excellent value compared to Western European capitals.

Booking Advice

For mid-range and fine dining restaurants, book in advance, especially for Friday and Saturday evenings. Most restaurants accept reservations via their website or by phone.

docs.title: Etno Dvaras
docs.text:
The most reliable spot for traditional Lithuanian food in the Old Town. Rustic wooden interior, generous portions, and a menu that covers all the classics.

  • Location: Pilies g. 16, Old Town
  • Price range: €€
  • Must order: Cepelinai, kugelis, dark rye bread basket
  • Hours: Daily 11:00–23:00

docs.title: Lokys (The Bear)
docs.text:
One of the oldest restaurants in Vilnius, operating since 1972 in a beautiful Gothic cellar. Specialises in game — wild boar, beaver, and venison alongside Lithuanian classics.

  • Location: Stikliu g. 8, Old Town
  • Price range: €€€
  • Must order: Wild boar stew, hunter's platter
  • Hours: Daily 12:00–24:00

docs.title: Senoji Kibininė
docs.text:
The best place in Vilnius for kibinai — the flaky Karaite pastries. Simple, casual, and excellent value.

  • Location: Bernardinu g. 9, Old Town
  • Price range: €
  • Must order: Lamb kibinas, honey beer
  • Hours: Daily 10:00–21:00

docs.title: Amandus
docs.text:
One of Vilnius's top fine dining restaurants, serving creative Lithuanian cuisine with high-quality seasonal ingredients. Elegant but not stuffy.

  • Location: Uzupio g. 20, Uzupis
  • Price range: €€€€
  • Must order: Tasting menu (changes seasonally)
  • Hours: Tue–Sat 18:00–23:00

docs.title: Sweet Root
docs.text:
Consistently ranked among the best restaurants in the Baltics. Creative, artistic tasting menus that showcase Lithuanian ingredients in unexpected ways.

  • Location: Uzupio g. 22, Uzupis
  • Price range: €€€€
  • Must order: Chef's tasting menu (advance booking essential)
  • Hours: Tue–Sat 18:00–22:00

docs.title: Nineteen18
docs.text:
Modern European cuisine in a beautifully designed space. Great cocktail bar attached.

  • Location: Gedimino pr. 13, New Town
  • Price range: €€€
  • Hours: Mon–Sat 12:00–23:00

docs.title: Halės Turgus (Hales Market)
docs.text:
Vilnius's main covered market, a short walk from the Old Town. Fresh produce, hot food stalls, and local snacks at very low prices. A great place to eat like a local.

  • Location: Pylimo g. 58
  • Price range: €
  • Hours: Mon–Sat 07:00–18:00, Sun 07:00–15:00

docs.title: Snekutis
docs.text:
A beloved neighbourhood bar and canteen serving cheap, hearty Lithuanian food and local beer. Cash only, no frills, completely authentic.

  • Location: Sv. Stepono g. 8, Uzupis
  • Price range: €
  • Hours: Mon–Thu 11:00–22:00, Fri–Sat 11:00–24:00

docs.title: Price Guide
docs.text: Symbol Price per person (food only) € Under €10 €€ €10–25 €€€ €25–50 €€€€ €50+

docs.title: Restaurant Map Overview
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Old Town] --> B[Etno Dvaras - Traditional]
    A --> C[Lokys - Game & Traditional]
    A --> D[Senoji Kibinine - Kibinai]
    E[Uzupis] --> F[Amandus - Fine Dining]
    E --> G[Sweet Root - Fine Dining]
    E --> H[Snekutis - Budget]
    I[New Town] --> J[Nineteen18 - Modern European]
    K[Hales Market] --> L[Various Budget Stalls]

docs.title: History of Vilnius
docs.text: Vilnius has been fought over, occupied, rebuilt, and reinvented more times than almost any other European capital. Understanding its history makes walking through it a completely different experience.

docs.title: Foundation (1323)
docs.text: Vilnius was founded by Grand Duke Gediminas, who according to legend dreamed of an iron wolf howling from a hill — interpreted as a sign to build a great city there. He wrote letters to European merchants and craftsmen inviting them to settle, making Vilnius an unusually cosmopolitan city from its earliest days.

docs.title: The Grand Duchy of Lithuania (14th–18th century)
docs.text:
At its peak, the Grand Duchy of Lithuania was one of the largest states in Europe, stretching from the Baltic to the Black Sea. Vilnius was its capital and cultural centre. The city's extraordinary architectural diversity — Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque all in one neighbourhood — comes from this long period of growth and wealth.

The Union of Lublin (1569) merged Lithuania with Poland into the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, which shaped the city's Polish cultural influences for centuries.

docs.title: Russian Empire (1795–1918)
docs.text: After the partitions of Poland, Vilnius fell under Russian control. The Russians called it Vilna. The city remained a major cultural centre — particularly for Jewish life. By the late 19th century, Vilnius was one of the most important centres of Jewish learning in the world, known as the "Jerusalem of Lithuania."

docs.title: The 20th Century — Occupation and Loss
docs.text:
The 20th century brought catastrophe. Vilnius changed hands repeatedly:

  • 1918 — Lithuania declares independence, claims Vilnius as capital
  • 1920 — Poland seizes Vilnius; Lithuania loses its own capital for nearly 20 years
  • 1939 — Soviet Union transfers Vilnius back to Lithuania under the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact
  • 1940 — Soviet occupation of Lithuania begins
  • 1941–1944 — German occupation; the Jewish community of Vilnius, numbering around 100,000 people, is almost entirely murdered
  • 1944–1990 — Soviet rule; the city is renamed Vilnius and industrialised; significant demographic changes as Lithuanians move in and Poles and others leave

docs.title: Independence (1990–present)
docs.text:
On 11 March 1990, Lithuania became the first Soviet republic to declare independence. Moscow responded with military force — in January 1991, Soviet troops attacked the Vilnius TV Tower, killing 14 civilians. Lithuanians formed a human chain to protect the parliament. The world watched. The Soviets backed down.

In September 1991, independence was internationally recognised. Lithuania joined the EU and NATO in 2004.

docs.title: Where to See It
docs.text:
• Museum of Occupations and Struggles — the former KGB headquarters, now a museum documenting Soviet and Nazi occupation
  • Paneriai Memorial — the site where 70,000–100,000 people, mostly Jewish, were murdered during the German occupation
  • Gediminas Tower — the symbol of the medieval Grand Duchy
  • Gate of Dawn — survived every occupation, has never been closed

docs.title: Language & Culture
docs.text: Lithuanian is one of the oldest living languages in the world — linguists study it because it preserves features of ancient Indo-European that other languages lost thousands of years ago. You are unlikely to learn it in a weekend, but a few words go a long way.

docs.title: Essential Phrases
docs.text: Lithuanian Pronunciation English Labas lah-bahs Hello Labas rytas lah-bahs ree-tahs Good morning Labas vakaras lah-bahs vah-kah-rahs Good evening Ačiū ah-choo Thank you Prašom prah-shom Please / You're welcome Atsiprašau aht-see-prah-show Excuse me / Sorry Taip tah-eep Yes Ne neh No Kiek kainuoja? kyek kai-nuo-yah How much does it cost? Kur yra...? koor ee-rah Where is...? Valgiaraštis val-gyah-rash-tis Menu Sąskaita sahs-kai-tah The bill Geras! geh-rahs Great! / Good! Sveikas! svay-kahs Cheers! (to a man) Sveika! svay-kah Cheers! (to a woman)

docs.title: Pronunciation Tips
docs.text:
• č sounds like "ch" in "church"
  • š sounds like "sh" in "shoe"
  • ž sounds like the "s" in "measure"
  • ū sounds like a long "oo"
  • ė sounds like "ay" in "say"

docs.title: Cultural Notes
docs.text:
Lithuanians are reserved at first. Do not mistake quietness for unfriendliness — once you get past the initial reserve, people are warm and generous. Smiling at strangers on the street is not the local custom.

Punctuality matters. If you are meeting someone, be on time. This applies to restaurant reservations too.

Amber is the national gemstone. You will see it everywhere. Baltic amber is among the finest in the world and is genuinely worth buying — just purchase from reputable shops rather than street stalls.

Nature is central to Lithuanian identity. Mushroom picking, berry foraging, and trips to the countryside are not tourist activities — they are what Lithuanians actually do on weekends. If someone invites you to their family's countryside house, accept.

docs.title: Useful Signs
docs.text: Lithuanian English Įėjimas Entrance Išėjimas Exit Atidarytas Open Uždarytas Closed Tualetas Toilet Kasa Cashier / Ticket office Draudžiama Prohibited

docs.title: Local Life
docs.text: Beyond the tourist sights, Vilnius has a rhythm of everyday life that is worth slowing down to notice.

docs.title: Halės Turgus (Hales Market)
docs.text:
The city's main covered market, a short walk from the Old Town on Pylimo Street. Open Monday to Saturday 07:00–18:00, Sunday until 15:00.

Come here to see what Lithuanians actually eat — smoked fish, enormous rounds of cheese, dark rye bread, fresh mushrooms in season, jars of forest honey. The hot food stalls serve cheap traditional lunches. This is one of the most authentic experiences in Vilnius.

docs.title: Kalvarijų Market
docs.text: A larger, more local market further from the centre. Less polished than Halės, more authentic. Worth visiting if you have extra time.

docs.title: Coffee Culture
docs.text:
Vilnius takes coffee seriously in a way that surprises most visitors. The city has produced European barista champions. The independent specialty cafe scene is genuinely world-class. Do not order coffee in a chain — find a local place instead.

See the Cafes & Coffee page for full recommendations.

docs.title: Seasonal Life
docs.text:
Summer — the city moves outside entirely. Every cafe has a terrace. Parks fill up. The Neris riverbank becomes a social hub. Festivals happen almost every weekend.

Mushroom season (August–October) — Lithuanians disappear to the forests. Restaurants feature fresh mushrooms on every menu. Markets overflow with chanterelles and porcini.

Christmas — Cathedral Square hosts one of the most beautiful Christmas markets in the Baltics. The city is quiet, cold, and genuinely magical in December.

docs.title: Trakai (45 minutes by train or bus)
docs.text:
A medieval castle on an island in the middle of a lake. One of the most photographed sights in Lithuania. Go for the castle, stay for the kibinai — the Karaite meat pastries that are only made here.

How to get there: Regular trains from Vilnius station (€1.50 each way). The castle is a 15-minute walk from the station.

docs.title: Kaunas (1 hour 20 minutes by bus or train)
docs.text: Lithuania's second city and the country's cultural capital during the interwar period. Excellent Art Deco architecture, a good Old Town, and the Devil Museum — a collection of over 3,000 devil figurines from around the world.

docs.title: Curonian Spit (4 hours by bus)
docs.text: A UNESCO-listed sand dune peninsula on the Baltic coast. Worth it for a longer trip — a minimum of two days.

docs.title: Events and Festivals
docs.text: Event When What Kaziukas Fair Early March Traditional craft fair, oldest in the region Vilnius Street Music Day June Free outdoor concerts across the city Vilnius City Days September Street festivals, concerts, citywide events Christmas Market December Cathedral Square, mulled wine, crafts

docs.title: Phrasebook
docs.text: A quick-reference card of Lithuanian phrases for your trip.

docs.title: Greetings
docs.text: Lithuanian English Labas Hello Labas rytas Good morning Labas vakaras Good evening Viso gero Goodbye Iki Bye (informal)

docs.title: Basics
docs.text: Lithuanian English Taip Yes Ne No Prašom Please / You're welcome Ačiū Thank you Labai ačiū Thank you very much Atsiprašau Excuse me / Sorry Gerai OK / Good

docs.title: Getting Around
docs.text: Lithuanian English Kur yra...? Where is...? Autobusų stotis Bus station Traukinių stotis Train station Oro uostas Airport Centras City centre Kairė Left Dešinė Right Tiesiai Straight ahead

docs.title: Eating & Drinking
docs.text: Lithuanian English Valgiaraštis Menu Sąskaita, prašom The bill, please Skanu Delicious Vanduo Water Alus Beer Vynas Wine Kava Coffee Be mėsos Without meat

docs.title: Emergencies
docs.text: Lithuanian English Pagalba! Help! Iškvieskite policiją Call the police Iškvieskite greitąją Call an ambulance Ligoninė Hospital Vaistinė Pharmacy

docs.title: Emergency Numbers
docs.text: Service Number Emergency (all services) 112 Police 112 Ambulance 112 Fire 112

docs.title: Useful Apps
docs.text:
Download these before you arrive.

App What it does Essential? Bolt Ride-hailing (like Uber, but dominant in Vilnius) Yes Trafi Public transport routes and real-time schedules Yes Google Maps Navigation — works excellently in Vilnius Yes Google Translate Lithuanian menus and signs Useful Bolt Food Food delivery Optional M-Ticket Cheaper bus/trolleybus tickets (€0.65 vs €1.00) If using public transport

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Bolt is the app to use for taxis in Lithuania. It is significantly cheaper than street taxis and you avoid any pricing disputes. Always use the app, never hail a taxi from the street.

Trafi covers all Vilnius buses and trolleybuses with real-time tracking. You can also buy tickets through the app.

M-Ticket — if you plan to use public transport regularly, download this and load it with credit. Tickets cost €0.65 instead of €1.00 cash, and a day pass is €3.00.
